english: 1. the high, cypress-like juniper-tree of the Himalaya mountains, the pencil cedar (Juniperus excelsa). It covers large mountain tracts, is considered sacred, and much used in religious ceremonies; its berries (śug-˳brás) (shug 'bras) are burnt as incense. — śug-dúd (shug dud) the smoke or perfume of juniper. — śug-tsér (shug tsher) Med. the young pointed sprouts of this tree. — śug-tsód (shug tshod) a sort of mistletoe, Viscum Oxycedri, growing on it and gradually killing it. The leaves have a slightly sour taste and are used for culinary purposes W. — rgya-śug (rgya shug) acc. to Cs. = spá-ma (spa ma) Juniperus squamosa, a low shrub and similar to our Juniperus communis. But a passage of the Stg. shows that its fruits are eaten like pease or rice, which cannot be imagined of juniper-berries or cypress cones; cf. spá-ma (spa ma). — 2. in śùg-pa (shug pa) ˳púd-pa ('pud pa) Sch., v. sub ˳púd-pa ('pud pa).

1. inherent strength, power, energy, c. genit.: dád-pai (dad pa'i), byáms-pai (byams pa'i), dgá-bai (dga' ba'i) śùgs-kyis (shugs kyis) by the power or ardour of faith, love, joy, e.g. to shed tears, = to weep with joy etc. Glr. and elsewh.; gcin-gyi (gcin gyi) śugs (shugs) dgag (dgag) mi (mi) bya (bya) the impulse to make water must not be suppressed Med.; ˳di-dag ('di dag) sṅón-gyi (sngon gyi) sbyín-śugs (sbyin shugs) yin (yin) this is the power of former alms or presents Glr.; túgs-rjei (thugs rje'i) śùgs-kyis (shugs kyis) by the power of grace Do.; der (der) sléb-pai (sleb pa'i) śugs (shugs) the power or ability of attaining to that place Thgr.; without a genit.: śùgs-kyis (shugs kyis) = ráṅ-śugs-kyis (rang shugs kyis) spontaneously, of one's own accord, śùgs-kyis (shugs kyis) yoṅ (yong) they will, no doubt, come of their own accord Mil.; śugs (shugs) byéd-pa (byed pa) to exert one's self(?); śugs-stóbs (shugs stobs) = śugs (shugs); sná-śugs (sna shugs) ˳drén-pa ('dren pa) Cs.: 'the accenting the first syllable'. — 2. col. also śubs (shubs) and śud (shud), mostly in compounds: śùgs-skad (shugs skad) Mil., śùgs-sgra (shugs sgra), col. *śùg-ra* (*shug ra*) a whistling, a whistle or whiff; śùgs-glu (shugs glu) 1. a whistling. 2. a whistled tune, *śùg-ḍa jhé'-pa* (*shug ḍa jhe' pa*) to whistle a tune C.; śùgs-pa (shugs pa) a small whistle which, in sounding it, is put quite into the mouth.

(W. *kóg-śùg* (*kog shug*)), śugs-ríṅ (shugs ring) sigh, groan, śugs (shugs) nar (nar) byéd-pa (byed pa) or ˳byín-pa ('byin pa) to sigh, to groan, śugs-riṅ (shugs ring) nar (nar) nár (nar) ˳dug ('dug) he heaves a deep sigh Mil. nt.
pf. śuṅs (shungs), 1. to snore. — 2. to hum, to buzz, e.g. of a large beetle.

pf. fut. bśud (bshud), 1. to rub, e.g. one thing against another C. — 2. to get scratched, excoriated, galled (cf. śùn-pa (shun pa)). — 3. śud (shud) byéd-pa (byed pa) (W. *čò-če* (*co ce*)) to steal silently away, to sneak off unperceived.

bark, rind, peel, skin, śun-kóg (shun kog), śun-págs (shun pags) id., the last expression is also used of the skin of animals Lex. — phyi-śun (phyi shun) the outer rind or skin, náṅ-śun (nang shun) the inner rind; bár-śun (bar shun) the middle rind, the bast, esp. of willows Sch.: śun-kóg (shun kog) láṅs-pa (langs pa) the spontaneous chapping or peeling off of the skin; śun-máṅ (shun mang) box-wood.

also śib-pa (shib pa), pf. imp. śubs (shubs), to speak in a low voice, to whisper, śub (shub) byéd-pa (byed pa) id.; *kog-śúb-la sil-če* (*kog shub la sil ce*) W. to read in a low voice, to read whispering; śùb-bu (shub bu) a whispering, śùb-bus (shub bus) zlá-ba (zla ba) to recite in a low voice Lex., śùb-bur (shub bur) smrá-ba (smra ba) B., *śúb-la zér-če* (*shub la zer ce*) W. to speak softly; śùb-bus (shub bus) smód-pa (smod pa) to reprehend in a whisper B.

case, covering, sheath, paper bag etc. frq.; rkaṅ-śúbs (rkang shubs), resp. żabs-śúbs (zhabs shubs) stocking, sock, gri-śùbs (gri shubs) knife-case or sheath, mje-śùbs (mje shubs) v. mje (mje); lag-śúbs (lag shubs), resp. phyag-śúbs (phyag shubs) glove.

pf. (b)śums ((b)shums), ft. bśum (bshum), imp. (b)śum(s) ((b)shum(s)), 1. to weep, ma (ma) śum (shum) mdzod (mdzod) do not weep! ṅu-śúm (ngu shum) Mil. weeping, lamentation. — 2. to tremble(?) graṅ-śùm (grang shum) Lt., Schr. graṅ-śùm (grang shum) byéd-pa (byed pa) to tremble or shiver with cold, to shudder.
pf. fut. bśur (bshur), imp. (b)śur ((b)shur), 1. to burn slightly, to singe. — 2. to cut off.
1. girdle, belt Lex.; śur-bu-phréu (shur bu phre'u) Zam. id. (acc. to Sch.). — 2. Cs.: sore, ulcer. — 3. Ts.: dumpling of flour, = ko-lag (ko lag).
1. an empty place, a place that has been left, that is no longer occupied, ráṅ-śul (rang shul) stóṅs-nas (stongs nas) your own place becoming
